model_summary: Targeting chronic shoulder/jaw tension and tension-linked headaches driven by persistent anticipatory threat and habitual bracing. Choose a compact BIO-PSYCHO-SOCIAL predictor set that (1) reduces peripheral muscle tension (PMR, ergonomics, micro-breaks, wearable cues), (2) increases early detection/interoception (body-scan), and (3) reduces threat expectation/habitual bracing through cognitive reappraisal and social support; sleep protection included as a systemic moderator. Measurements combine 5x/day momentary EMA (1–9 Likert) + 1x/day morning diary for sleep + passive wearable bruxism/jaw-clench signal aggregated per prompt and daily. This design preserves the complaint's resolution (C01–C06), is estimable for gVAR with ~140 momentary observations across 28 days, and minimizes redundant predictors while covering main mechanisms.
variable_selection_notes: Selected criteria (C01–C06) follow the complaint decomposition to preserve distinct constructs: muscle-level tension (C01), interoceptive awareness (C02), headache events (C03), anticipatory threat (C04), relaxation ability in low-stress contexts (C05), and habitual bracing (C06). Predictors were chosen from high-ranking HyDe/LLM signals emphasizing mindfulness/body-scan, PMR, breathing/micro-breaks, cognitive reappraisal, ergonomics, sleep, wearables for bruxism, and social support. Trade-offs: kept both PMR and micro-breaks (different dose/frequency profiles) but avoided adding many overlapping downregulation techniques (e.g., multiple breathing variants) to limit collinearity. Uncertainty/bias: passive jaw-clench sensor availability varies by user; self-report measures rely on subjective scaling. Prioritize within-person 1–9 Likert items to maximize variance for gVAR.

momentary_ema.notes: Use small jitter within each block to avoid predictability. Prompts ask core criteria + selected predictor adherence/intensity items. Average completion target >= 70% prompts.

daily_diary.notes: Morning diary captures prior-night sleep quality, sleep duration, and global baseline for the day (sleep protection adherence).

passive_streams.notes: If wearable with bruxism/jaw-clench detection is available, aggregate clench-episode counts and average intensity in the 'since last prompt' window; also provide daily totals. If no wearable, fall back to EMA self-report bracing flags.

design_recommendations.gvar_readiness_notes: Target ~140 momentary samples; after quality control aim for >=100 usable timepoints. Expect nonstationarity (linear trends or learning effects); detrend (person-specific linear detrending) then within-person z-scoring before gVAR. For count/zero-inflated headache signals use log1p or two-part models (event flag + intensity).
design_recommendations.variance_support_notes: Use 1–9 Likert for momentary constructs to maximize within-person variance. For headache use two-field approach (binary event since-last-prompt + intensity 1–9 if event). Passive clench counts expected to provide additional variance for bracing detection.
design_recommendations.missingness_risk_notes: Main risks: prompt fatigue, wearable dropout, sensor pairing issues. Mitigation: brief items, adherence nudges, allow short fallback self-report for passive signals, and imputation strategies (last-observation-carried-forward not recommended; use model-based interpolation).

safety_notes: No acute safety concerns signaled in the complaint. If during data collection the participant reports escalating severe or disabling headaches, severe functional impairment, or suicidal ideation, advise immediate clinical contact. Ensure explicit consent and privacy protections for any passive physiological monitoring (P07).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person linear detrend then z-score; treat as approx_continuous (1-9)

predictor_variables.label: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) practice (shoulders/jaw-focused)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Scheduled PMR trains peripheral muscle downregulation and provides a competing relaxation response to habitual bracing, reducing baseline tension and downstream headache triggers.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Daily 10–20 minute guided shoulders/jaw PMR sessions; short 3–5 minute targeted PMR micro-sessions.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you do a PMR/targeted muscle relaxation practice? If yes, estimate total minutes and rate how fully you engaged (1-9).
measurement.notes: Capture both occurrence (adherence) and engagement/intensity. Use summed minutes per day for dose-response.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: minutes: log1p for skew; engagement: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: PMR reduces peripheral muscle tension within minutes to hours.
Reduced muscle tension decreases tension-type headache probability over hours.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility via brief EMA; self-report minutes may be noisy.

predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Frequent short breaks reduce accumulated muscle tension and autonomic arousal; combined movement prevents postural stiffness while breathing downshifts arousal.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: 1–3 minute standing stretch + 6-breath diaphragmatic breathing every hour; phone reminders to cue micro-breaks.

measurement.notes: Short frequent actions expected to show immediate (0-6h) effects on tension and relaxation.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: counts: square-root or log1p; effectiveness z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Micro-breaks quickly increase ability to relax and reduce stiffness.

feasibility.notes: Feasible; may rely on user action and reminders for adherence.

Also enables cue-based interventions. | Intervention examples: If wearable detects clench episodes, trigger brief cue to release jaw and breathe; use daily summary to inform habit-change. | Measurement: wearable_sensor | Number of clench/bracing episodes and average intensity in the 'since last prompt' window; daily total episodes and mean intensity. | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Personalization_Monitoring_and_Data_Feedback / Wearables_and_Home_Monitoring / muscle_tension / jaw_clench_bruxism_sensor

predictor_variables.label: Structured social support (brief weekly check-ins / encouraging cues)
predictor_variables.mechanism_rationale: Supportive social contact can reduce chronic anticipatory threat and increase coping resources, indirectly reducing bracing and muscle tension.
predictor_variables.intervention_examples_safe: Weekly scheduled short check-ins with supportive person(s) or brief supportive messages/calls; encouragement to practice relaxation skills.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (weekly check-in + momentary prompts)
measurement.item_or_signal: In the last day(s), did you have a supportive check-in (yes/no)? Rate how supported you felt (1-9).
measurement.notes: Social support more slowly varying; can be used as moderator or distal predictor.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: flag + z-score for perceived support

expected_effects.rationale: Support reduces perceived threat; effects may accumulate over days.

feasibility.notes: Useful as moderator; lower within-person variance expected on short timescales.

predictor_variables.mapped_rationale: Variable label Structured social support (brief weekly check-ins / encouraging cues) maps to SOCIAL / social_support_and_belonging / support_network_development / family_caregiver_and_friends_mobilization / supportive_checkin_network_setup. This leaf explicitly represents a structured check-in network setup with family and friends, aligning with the notion of brief weekly check-ins. It is the most specific operational construct among candidates that matches the intended predictor (structured social support through check-ins). The measurement design (EMA self-report with binary yes/no for a supportive check-in plus a 1–9 felt support rating, 5 prompts per day) fits a network-based check-in mechanism as a moderator/distal predictor within the model. This aligns with the pseudoprofile aims to harness social support to reduce anticipatory threat and habitual bracing.

edges.notes: PMR expected to acutely reduce muscle tension.
Higher wearable clench counts indicate greater habitual bracing (measurement edge).
Micro-breaks rapidly improve ability to relax.
Cognitive reappraisal lowers anticipatory threat expectation over hours to a day.
Ergonomic changes reduce tonic muscle loading and tension.
Improved sleep reduces headache susceptibility the following day.
PMR can increase relaxation ability on the same day.
Improved awareness enables earlier corrective action reducing tension.
Supportive contact reduces perceived threat across days.

expected_collinearity_risks: C01 (muscle tension) and C06 (habitual bracing) — high collinearity risk
P01 (PMR) and P03 (micro-breaks) — medium collinearity (both downregulation tools)
P01 and P02 — moderate correlation if body-scan is used before/after PMR
expected_floor_ceiling_risks: P07 passive sensor counts — potential floor (no device / low events) or ceiling with noisy artifact if device misclassifies
Perceived social support (P08) may cluster at high end for some individuals (ceiling)
variance_enhancement_suggestions: Use 1–9 Likert anchors for momentary constructs to increase variance.
Collect both event flag + intensity for headaches to reduce binary floor effect.
Use passive clench counts when available to increase objective variance for bracing.
excluded_or_deprioritized_candidates: High-complexity neuromodulation/medical interventions (TMS, implantables) — deprioritized for initial gVAR due to feasibility and safety complexity.
Multiple overlapping breathing modalities — consolidated into micro-break breathing to reduce redundancy.
Medication dosing or pharmacotherapy details — excluded (beyond scope and safety).
diagnostics.gvar_feasibility_summary: With 5 prompts/day over 28 days (~140 momentary observations) and a compact set of 6 criteria + 8 predictors, the design is estimable for person-level gVAR after detrending and z-scoring. Expect to drop variables with extensive missingness (e.g., P07 if wearable unavailable). Use two-part modelling for headache signal.

criteria.label: Elevated resting muscle tension in shoulders and jaw
criteria.query_text_used: Elevated resting muscle tension in shoulders and jaw | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how tense were your shoulders and jaw? (1 = not at all tense, 9 = extremely tense) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Primary momentary outcome. Also complemented by passive bruxism/clench sensor when available.

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf is neck_muscle_tension_associated_with_tmjd. The var refers to Elevated resting muscle tension in shoulders and jaw, captured via momentary EMA (1–9) self-report. Among candidates, this leaf explicitly encodes muscle tension in neck/jaw region linked to temporomandibular joint disorders (TMJD), aligning with the C01 muscle tension construct and the biomechanical/occlusal domain relevant to jaw/shoulder tension. Other leaves either address sleep bruxism (sleep-related measures), tenderness to palpation, or non-specific pain descriptors. This is the most specific craniofacial/neck musculature tension construct available, fitting the intended mechanism while remaining compatible with within-person EMA across 5 prompts per day.

source_variable.label: Elevated resting muscle tension in shoulders and jaw

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how tense were your shoulders and jaw? (1 = not at all tense, 9 = extremely tense)
measurement.notes: Primary momentary outcome. Also complemented by passive bruxism/clench sensor when available.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person linear detrend then z-score; treat as approx_continuous (1-9)

criteria.label: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension
criteria.query_text_used: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how aware were you of bodily tension or tightness (shoulders, jaw or neck)? (1 = not at all aware, 9 = very aware) | Polarity: higher_is_better | Notes: Interoceptive awareness; higher scores are salutogenic. Important mediator for early detection.

mapping.rationale: Variable C02 corresponds to interoceptive/body awareness of muscular tension. Among candidates, idx 3913 explicitly encodes 'listening to body signals', 'body awareness', and 'body mindfulness'—the most specific operational construct for interoceptive awareness of bodily tension in EMA. The 1–9 Likert EMA item fits framing of awareness magnitude; daily diary context aligns with within-person fluctuations. Therefore this leaf best matches the intended construct.

source_variable.label: Low moment-to-moment awareness of bodily tension

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how aware were you of bodily tension or tightness (shoulders, jaw or neck)? (1 = not at all aware, 9 = very aware)
measurement.notes: Interoceptive awareness; higher scores are salutogenic. Important mediator for early detection.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider modelling as continuous

criteria.label: Headaches triggered by muscular tension
criteria.query_text_used: Headaches triggered by muscular tension | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, did you have a headache? (Yes/No). If yes, rate intensity: 1 = very mild, 9 = very severe. | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Expect zero-inflation (many no-headache prompts). Use two-part modelling or log1p intensity for gVAR.

mapping.rationale: Best match for variable C03: Headaches triggered by muscular tension. The candidate leaf 16165 represents tension-type headache with mild_to_moderate_intensity, aligning with the measurement item (EMA: binary event + intensity 1-9) and the mechanism where headaches are associated with muscle tension. This is more specific to the described construct than other headache categories and fits the 1–9 intensity scale used in the pseudo-profile. Supports C01 (muscle tension) and C03 (headache events) within the intended biopsychosocial predictor framework.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you have a headache? (Yes/No). If yes, rate intensity: 1 = very mild, 9 = very severe.
measurement.notes: Expect zero-inflation (many no-headache prompts). Use two-part modelling or log1p intensity for gVAR.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: model binary event separately; log1p(intensity) or ordinal modelling for intensity

criteria.label: Persistent anticipatory threat expectation
criteria.query_text_used: Persistent anticipatory threat expectation | Operational item/signal: Right now / Since the last prompt, how much did you expect something bad to happen? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Key psychological driver of habitual bracing; target for cognitive reappraisal interventions.

mapping.rationale: The variable C04 (Persistent anticipatory threat expectation) maps best to a generalized anticipatory worry/cognition about future threat. Among candidates, idx 32054 corresponds to a generalized anxiety construct with cognitive rumination and future-oriented worry/rumination, which aligns with the EMA item asking how much something bad is expected to happen since the last prompt (1–9). It is the most specific candidate that captures anticipatory threat expectancy in a general sense, rather than highly context-specific anticipatory anxieties (e.g., driving, social evaluative contexts) or other threat-related constructs (startle, catastrophizing, etc.). The measurement mode (EMA_self_report) and scale (1–9) fit this construct well and support within-person variance needed for gVAR.

measurement.item_or_signal: Right now / Since the last prompt, how much did you expect something bad to happen? (1 = not at all, 9 = extremely)
measurement.notes: Key psychological driver of habitual bracing; target for cognitive reappraisal interventions.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider autoregressive components in gVAR

criteria.label: Difficulty relaxing on low-stress days
criteria.query_text_used: Difficulty relaxing on low-stress days | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how hard was it for you to relax (physically or mentally)? (1 = not at all hard, 9 = extremely hard) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Also capture perceived situational stress in the same prompt (single extra item) to identify 'low-stress' contexts for subgroup analyses.

mapping.rationale: Variable label 'Difficulty relaxing on low-stress days' corresponds to an emotional regulation target focused on reducing bodily/mental tension. The candidate leaf 5709 is in the emotional domain: emotional regulation / emotional stability / anxiety reduction / reducing tension. It aligns with EMA self-report item about how hard it is to relax and captures the low-stress context as described in the pseudoprofile. It is the most specific among the relaxation-focused leaves that centers on tension reduction rather than generic stress-management techniques, sleep, or broader coping strategies.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how hard was it for you to relax (physically or mentally)? (1 = not at all hard, 9 = extremely hard)
measurement.notes: Also capture perceived situational stress in the same prompt (single extra item) to identify 'low-stress' contexts for subgroup analyses.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; interact with perceived situational stress variable to identify low-stress contexts

criteria.label: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat
criteria.query_text_used: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat | Operational item/signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you notice automatic bracing/tensing (muscles activating without a clear reason)? (1 = none, 9 = constant unintended bracing) | Polarity: higher_is_worse | Notes: Complement with passive jaw-clench sensor counts when available to reduce self-report bias.

mapping.rationale: Variable C06 targets habitual anticipatory muscle bracing (automatic, non-purposeful tension). Among candidates, bruxism_behavior (jaw bracing/clenching) best represents a concrete, habitual muscle-tension manifestation that often occurs outside conscious control and aligns with the EMA emphasis on automatic, non-deliberate muscle activity. The measurement item (1–9 Likert for noticing automatic bracing) maps to the degree of such habitual bracing, and the note to complement with passive jaw-clench sensor data directly supports objective corroboration of this bracing pattern. While bruxism is jaw-specific, it captures the core mechanism (habitual, anticipatory muscle tension) central to C06 more precisely than other leaves, making it the best single match among candidates.

source_variable.label: Habitual anticipatory muscle bracing in absence of immediate threat

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, how much did you notice automatic bracing/tensing (muscles activating without a clear reason)? (1 = none, 9 = constant unintended bracing)
measurement.notes: Complement with passive jaw-clench sensor counts when available to reduce self-report bias.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: within-person z-score; consider modelling alongside passive clench counts to separate perception vs behavior

predictors.label: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) practice (shoulders/jaw-focused)
predictors.query_text_used: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) practice (shoulders/jaw-focused) | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Scheduled PMR trains peripheral muscle downregulation and provides a competing relaxation response to habitual bracing, reducing baseline tension and downstream headache triggers. | Intervention examples: Daily 10–20 minute guided shoulders/jaw PMR sessions; short 3–5 minute targeted PMR micro-sessions. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you do a PMR/targeted muscle relaxation practice? If yes, estimate total minutes and rate how fully you engaged (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / breath_and_relaxation_skills / progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice

mapping.rationale: Best-matching leaf for PMR practice: progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice exactly matches the variable label. The leaf is located under PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / physiological_downshift / breath_and_relaxation_skills, aligning with PMR as a relaxation technique targeting peripheral muscle tension. Measurement mode EMA_self_report with occurrence/adherence + minutes + engagement (1–9) fits the dose/engagement data described. Mechanism rationale aligns with scheduled PMR reducing baseline muscle tension and habitual bracing, thus lowering headache triggers. This leaf provides the most specific operational construct for PMR within the psycho-social predictor set in the pseudoprofile context.
mapping.leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / physiological_downshift / breath_and_relaxation_skills / progressive_muscle_relaxation_practice

source_variable.label: Progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) practice (shoulders/jaw-focused)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Scheduled PMR trains peripheral muscle downregulation and provides a competing relaxation response to habitual bracing, reducing baseline tension and downstream headache triggers.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Daily 10–20 minute guided shoulders/jaw PMR sessions; short 3–5 minute targeted PMR micro-sessions.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you do a PMR/targeted muscle relaxation practice? If yes, estimate total minutes and rate how fully you engaged (1-9).
measurement.notes: Capture both occurrence (adherence) and engagement/intensity. Use summed minutes per day for dose-response.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: minutes: log1p for skew; engagement: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: PMR reduces peripheral muscle tension within minutes to hours.
Reduced muscle tension decreases tension-type headache probability over hours.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility via brief EMA; self-report minutes may be noisy.

predictors.label: Interoceptive body-scan / mindful-body awareness check-ins
predictors.query_text_used: Interoceptive body-scan / mindful-body awareness check-ins | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Improves moment-to-moment detection of tension (increasing C02), enabling earlier self-regulation and reducing downstream bracing/headache episodes. | Intervention examples: Brief guided 1–3 minute body-checks deployed as a notification or self-initiated body-scan practice. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you do a body awareness or body-scan check-in? If yes, how long (minutes) and how attentive did you feel (1-9)? | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / body_scan_with_breath_integration

mapping.rationale: The variable label Interoceptive body-scan / mindful-body awareness check-ins maps exactly to the leaf body_scan_with_breath_integration under PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / mindfulness_based_interventions / body_based_mindfulness. The measurement item asks about performing a body awareness or body-scan check-in (with minutes and attention), which aligns with the breath-integrated body scan practice. This leaf is the most specific operational construct for interoceptive body scanning within the provided candidates, matching the mechanism of improving moment-to-moment interoceptive detection to regulate tension and reduce bracing/headache episodes. The pseudoprofile context supports EMA-based monitoring at 5 prompts/day and body-scan interventions, consistent with this predictor.

source_variable.label: Interoceptive body-scan / mindful-body awareness check-ins
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Improves moment-to-moment detection of tension (increasing C02), enabling earlier self-regulation and reducing downstream bracing/headache episodes.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief guided 1–3 minute body-checks deployed as a notification or self-initiated body-scan practice.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you do a body awareness or body-scan check-in? If yes, how long (minutes) and how attentive did you feel (1-9)?
measurement.notes: Key proximal predictor of increased interoceptive awareness (C02).

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: minutes: log1p; attention: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Body-scan increases awareness of bodily signals within minutes to hours.

feasibility.notes: High within-person variance expected; adherence may decline without reminders.

predictors.label: Hourly micro-breaks with gentle movement + diaphragmatic breathing
predictors.query_text_used: Hourly micro-breaks with gentle movement + diaphragmatic breathing | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Frequent short breaks reduce accumulated muscle tension and autonomic arousal; combined movement prevents postural stiffness while breathing downshifts arousal. | Intervention examples: 1–3 minute standing stretch + 6-breath diaphragmatic breathing every hour; phone reminders to cue micro-breaks. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you take a micro-break (movement + breathing)? If yes, how many times and rate effectiveness (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / third_wave_and_contextual_therapies / stress_inoculation_and_coping_skills / arousal_management_skills

mapping.rationale: Variable label: Hourly micro-breaks with gentle movement + diaphragmatic breathing. The closest and most specific ontology leaf is diaphragmatic_breathing_practice (leaf 3055) under breath_and_relaxation_skills, which directly corresponds to the diaphragmatic breathing component of the intervention. The measurement item asks about micro-breaks (movement + breathing) and their effectiveness, matching the breath-focused relaxation mechanism that reduces muscle tension and autonomic arousal. Although movement is not explicitly captured by this leaf, diaphragmatic breathing_practice is the most specific, mechanistically aligned predictor available among the candidates for the breathing aspect of the micro-breaks in this BIOxPSYCHO context.
mapping.leaf_full_path: PSYCHO / core_psychological_skills_toolkits / stress_resilience_and_downregulation / physiological_downshift / breath_and_relaxation_skills / diaphragmatic_breathing_practice

source_variable.label: Hourly micro-breaks with gentle movement + diaphragmatic breathing
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Frequent short breaks reduce accumulated muscle tension and autonomic arousal; combined movement prevents postural stiffness while breathing downshifts arousal.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: 1–3 minute standing stretch + 6-breath diaphragmatic breathing every hour; phone reminders to cue micro-breaks.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you take a micro-break (movement + breathing)? If yes, how many times and rate effectiveness (1-9).
measurement.notes: Short frequent actions expected to show immediate (0-6h) effects on tension and relaxation.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: counts: square-root or log1p; effectiveness z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Micro-breaks quickly increase ability to relax and reduce stiffness.

feasibility.notes: Feasible; may rely on user action and reminders for adherence.

predictors.label: Threat reappraisal / cognitive reframing practice
predictors.query_text_used: Threat reappraisal / cognitive reframing practice | Domain: PSYCHO | Mechanism: Active cognitive reappraisal reduces persistent anticipatory threat expectations, thereby reducing mental tension and secondary bracing. | Intervention examples: Brief 1–2 question reappraisal prompts (e.g., probability and coping re-evaluation) and short cognitive exercises to consider alternative, less-threatening interpretations. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you try a reappraisal/cognitive reframing exercise? If yes, rate how helpful it felt (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / threat_and_anxiety / threat_appraisal_reframe

mapping.rationale: The variable Threat reappraisal / cognitive reframing practice directly maps to a leaf focused on threat_appraisal_reframe within cognitive restructuring (PSYCHO / cognitive_behavioral_therapies / cognitive_restructuring / emotion_linked_reappraisal / threat_and_anxiety / threat_appraisal_reframe). This is the most specific operational construct among candidates for modeling threat reinterpretation or reappraisal prompts. The measurement item (Did you try a reappraisal/cognitive reframing exercise? with a 1–9 helpfulness scale) aligns with a cognitive reappraisal practice indicator in EMA. The overall psycho-social mechanism matches the intention to reduce anticipatory threat and habitual bracing, and the context supports brief reappraisal prompts as safe intervention examples. Therefore this leaf best captures Threat reappraisal within the specified predictor set.

source_variable.label: Threat reappraisal / cognitive reframing practice
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Active cognitive reappraisal reduces persistent anticipatory threat expectations, thereby reducing mental tension and secondary bracing.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Brief 1–2 question reappraisal prompts (e.g., probability and coping re-evaluation) and short cognitive exercises to consider alternative, less-threatening interpretations.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you try a reappraisal/cognitive reframing exercise? If yes, rate how helpful it felt (1-9).
measurement.notes: Expected to change anticipatory threat over hours to days; effect size on muscle tension likely indirect.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: binary flag as indicator; helpfulness z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Reappraisal directly targets threat expectation; effects may consolidate across prompts.

feasibility.notes: Adherence may be lower than simple breathing prompts; include micro-exercises to improve uptake.

predictors.label: Ergonomic / posture cueing and adjustments
predictors.query_text_used: Ergonomic / posture cueing and adjustments | Domain: BIOxPSYCHO | Mechanism: Postural improvements and cueing reduce mechanical loading on neck/shoulders and reduce tonic muscle tension and bracing. | Intervention examples: Adjust chair/monitor height, use posture reminder app, brief posture reset exercises hourly. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Since the last prompt, did you adjust posture or follow an ergonomic cue (yes/no)? If yes, rate how well you implemented it (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Movement_Physical_Function_and_Somatic_Care / Ergonomics_Posture_and_Work_Capacity

mapping.rationale: Selected leaf is standing_posture_cue_tool, the most specific ergonomic cueing device under Ergonomics_Posture_and_Work_Capacity aligned with P05 (Ergonomic / posture cueing and adjustments). Measurement item (yes/no of adjusting posture or following an ergonomic cue plus implementation rating 1–9) fits cueing tools and supports within-person variability needed for EMA. This provides the exact operational construct for posture cueing interventions within the BIO domain, consistent with the pseudoprofile's aims to reduce tonic muscle tension via cues. The context mentions passive posture proxies as optional, but the primary predictor here is the cueing tool itself.

source_variable.label: Ergonomic / posture cueing and adjustments
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Postural improvements and cueing reduce mechanical loading on neck/shoulders and reduce tonic muscle tension and bracing.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Adjust chair/monitor height, use posture reminder app, brief posture reset exercises hourly.

measurement.item_or_signal: Since the last prompt, did you adjust posture or follow an ergonomic cue (yes/no)? If yes, rate how well you implemented it (1-9).
measurement.notes: Optional passive posture proxy via phone orientation/accelerometer if available — aggregate per-prompt.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: flag + quality z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Mechanical/postural changes reduce sustained muscle tension over hours.

feasibility.notes: Feasible but may rely on environmental changes; passive posture data optional.

predictors.label: Sleep protection / circadian routine adherence
predictors.query_text_used: Sleep protection / circadian routine adherence | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Improved sleep quality reduces baseline arousal, lowers headache susceptibility, and improves daytime relaxation and resilience. | Intervention examples: Consistent bed/wake times, pre-sleep downshift (no screens 30–60 min), short presleep PMR. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | Last night: total sleep time (hrs), sleep quality (1-9), time to fall asleep in minutes. Also capture adherence to planned sleep routine (yes/no). | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Sleep_Circadian_and_Restoration / Sleep_Routines_and_Practices

mapping.rationale: Variable P06 targets Sleep protection and circadian routine adherence, with EMA-based morning diary capturing last night’s sleep metrics: total sleep time, sleep quality, time to fall asleep, and adherence to planned sleep routine. The candidate leaf 4854 corresponds to BIO / circadian_and_sleep_tracking / sleep_diary, the exact operational construct for self-reported daily sleep data. This leaf is the most specific match for the measurement mode (EMA_self_report) and items (sleep duration, sleep quality, onset latency, routine adherence) described in the variable, aligning well with the pseudoprofile’s sleep-protection mechanism.

source_variable.label: Sleep protection / circadian routine adherence
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Improved sleep quality reduces baseline arousal, lowers headache susceptibility, and improves daytime relaxation and resilience.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Consistent bed/wake times, pre-sleep downshift (no screens 30–60 min), short presleep PMR.

measurement.item_or_signal: Last night: total sleep time (hrs), sleep quality (1-9), time to fall asleep in minutes. Also capture adherence to planned sleep routine (yes/no).
measurement.notes: Moderator variable; daily morning entry reduces recall bias for sleep.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: sleep time: z-score or raw hours; quality: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Better sleep reduces headache risk the following day; effects may appear overnight and in daytime.

feasibility.notes: High feasibility via single daily diary item.

predictors.label: Wearable jaw-clench/bruxism sensor (passive) — episodes & intensity
predictors.query_text_used: Wearable jaw-clench/bruxism sensor (passive) — episodes & intensity | Domain: BIO | Mechanism: Objective detection of clench/bracing episodes provides early warning (improves C02 via feedback) and a direct behavioral measure of habitual bracing (C06 & C01). Also enables cue-based interventions. | Intervention examples: If wearable detects clench episodes, trigger brief cue to release jaw and breathe; use daily summary to inform habit-change. | Measurement: wearable_sensor | Number of clench/bracing episodes and average intensity in the 'since last prompt' window; daily total episodes and mean intensity. | Prior ontology hint: BIO / Personalization_Monitoring_and_Data_Feedback / Wearables_and_Home_Monitoring / muscle_tension / jaw_clench_bruxism_sensor

mapping.rationale: Selected leaf 4564 because it exactly maps to BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / neurophysiology_and_stress_proxies / muscle_tension / jaw_clench_bruxism_sensor, which corresponds to a passive wearable jaw-clench/bruxism sensor capturing episodes and average intensity. This aligns with the variable's label (Wearable jaw-clench/bruxism sensor), measurement mode (passive_aggregated_per_prompt), and measurement item (number of clench/bracing episodes and mean intensity). It provides an objective measure of habitual bracing (C06) and directly reflects muscle tension (C01) while enabling feedback-driven interventions. Other options (e.g., sleep-oriented mouthguard devices or general sleep wearables) are less specific to jaw clenching, making this the most precise operational match.
mapping.leaf_full_path: BIO / personalization_monitoring_and_data_feedback / wearables_and_home_monitoring / physiology / neurophysiology_and_stress_proxies / muscle_tension / jaw_clench_bruxism_sensor

source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Objective detection of clench/bracing episodes provides early warning (improves C02 via feedback) and a direct behavioral measure of habitual bracing (C06 & C01). Also enables cue-based interventions.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: If wearable detects clench episodes, trigger brief cue to release jaw and breathe; use daily summary to inform habit-change.

measurement.channel: wearable device with jaw-clench or EMG proxy (or night bruxism sensor); phone-connected wearable
measurement.item_or_signal: Number of clench/bracing episodes and average intensity in the 'since last prompt' window; daily total episodes and mean intensity.
measurement.response_scale_or_unit: count of episodes; mean intensity (device-specific units)
measurement.notes: If unavailable, fallback to EMA self-report bracing counts. Ensure timestamps aligned with EMA prompts for per-prompt aggregation.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: counts: square-root or log1p; intensity: z-score

expected_effects.rationale: Higher detected clench counts correspond to greater habitual bracing; wearable is a direct behavioral measure.
Providing feedback can increase awareness; passive signal itself helps validate self-report.

source_variable.risk_ethics_notes: Ensure explicit consent for passive EMG/bruxism monitoring; privacy of physiological data must be protected.

feasibility.notes: Dependent on user's device availability and pairing; may be missing for many participants.

predictors.label: Structured social support (brief weekly check-ins / encouraging cues)
predictors.query_text_used: Structured social support (brief weekly check-ins / encouraging cues) | Domain: SOCIAL | Mechanism: Supportive social contact can reduce chronic anticipatory threat and increase coping resources, indirectly reducing bracing and muscle tension. | Intervention examples: Weekly scheduled short check-ins with supportive person(s) or brief supportive messages/calls; encouragement to practice relaxation skills. | Measurement: EMA_self_report | In the last day(s), did you have a supportive check-in (yes/no)? Rate how supported you felt (1-9). | Prior ontology hint: SOCIAL / Social_Support_and_Belonging / Support_Network_Development

mapping.rationale: Variable label Structured social support (brief weekly check-ins / encouraging cues) maps to SOCIAL / social_support_and_belonging / support_network_development / family_caregiver_and_friends_mobilization / supportive_checkin_network_setup. This leaf explicitly represents a structured check-in network setup with family and friends, aligning with the notion of brief weekly check-ins. It is the most specific operational construct among candidates that matches the intended predictor (structured social support through check-ins). The measurement design (EMA self-report with binary yes/no for a supportive check-in plus a 1–9 felt support rating, 5 prompts per day) fits a network-based check-in mechanism as a moderator/distal predictor within the model. This aligns with the pseudoprofile aims to harness social support to reduce anticipatory threat and habitual bracing.

source_variable.label: Structured social support (brief weekly check-ins / encouraging cues)
source_variable.mechanism_rationale: Supportive social contact can reduce chronic anticipatory threat and increase coping resources, indirectly reducing bracing and muscle tension.
source_variable.intervention_examples_safe: Weekly scheduled short check-ins with supportive person(s) or brief supportive messages/calls; encouragement to practice relaxation skills.

measurement.channel: smartphone EMA app (weekly check-in + momentary prompts)
measurement.item_or_signal: In the last day(s), did you have a supportive check-in (yes/no)? Rate how supported you felt (1-9).
measurement.notes: Social support more slowly varying; can be used as moderator or distal predictor.

gvar_handling.suggested_transformation: flag + z-score for perceived support

expected_effects.rationale: Support reduces perceived threat; effects may accumulate over days.

feasibility.notes: Useful as moderator; lower within-person variance expected on short timescales.
